column2: A small house in a grove by the Nakagawa River, home to a woman Genji had once known, which he passes on his way to Hanachirusato in Hanachirusato (Ch11, 'The Lady at the Villa of Scattering Orange Blossoms'). Hearing 'the lovely strains of a koto tuned to the rustic Azuma mode' from within, he 'realized that he had once visited the woman who lived there' (Ch11, p.223); a cuckoo calls 'as though the bird was inviting him inside,' and he sends Koremitsu in with a poem (Ch11, p.223). The two poems (11xx) are this cuckoo-prompted overture — which the woman coolly rebuffs. A brief roadside prelude to the chapter's main visit; cf. 'The Reikeiden Consort's villa.'

The Akashi shore at the moment of Genji's pardon and departure for the capital, in Akashi (Ch13, 'The Lady at Akashi'). As the day of departure nears, Genji's parting from the now-pregnant Akashi lady centers on music: he asks her to 'play one song on the koto for me as a keepsake for my heart' (Ch13, p.274) and, exchanging vows, gives her his own instrument — 'Take this koto as a memento until the time when we will play together again' (Ch13, p.274), the koto-keepsake verse. On the grand departure day the Priest presents Genji with hunting robes to which the lady has attached her farewell poem (Ch13, p.275). The six poems are this poignant leave-taking, weighted with Genji's promise that he will one day send for the lady and their daughter.

A deserted, ruined mansion to which Genji secretly takes Yūgao, and where she dies, in Yūgao (Ch4, 'The Lady of the Evening Faces'). Approaching at dawn, they gaze at 'the shinobugusa ferns... shooting up in a wild tangle beneath a weathered gate,' 'the thick shade of overgrown trees' making the scene 'indescribably gloomy' (Ch4, p.67); the garden is 'overgrown and desolate,' the trees 'weirdly disturbing,' the pond 'choked with weeds' — 'dark, deserted, and a little terrifying' (Ch4, p.68). 'An eerie place,' Genji says, 'but I don't mind. No demons or spirits will trouble me' (Ch4, p.68) — words the night cruelly refutes when a spirit (implicitly the Rokujō lady's) appears and Yūgao dies in his arms. The four poems (04xx) belong to this most haunted of the tale's settings.

column1: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
column2: The night sea-flight by which Tamakazura's party escapes Kyūshū and the Audit Officer's forced marriage, in Tamakazura (Ch22, 'A Lovely Garland'). Having 'fled Kyūshū for the capital' by ship (Ch22, p.411), they pass 'the beach in front of the Matsuura Shrine' as the younger sister looks 'back in sorrow' at the family left behind; a poem rows them 'away from Ukishima, isle of woe' (Ch22, p.411). In 'a speedy boat' they race the waves, fearing the warlord's pursuit, until reaching the mouth of the Yodo River at Kawajiri brings relief 'as if they had come back to life' (Ch22). The three poems (22xx) belong to this tense, grief-laced flight home — the voyage that returns Tamakazura to the world of the capital.
column3: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital

column1: En route from Uji to the capital (Naka no Kimi's move)
column2: The night road from Uji to the Nijō villa, traveled by Naka no Kimi when she moves to the capital as Niou's wife, in Sawarabi (Ch48, 'Early Fiddlehead Greens'). As the escort urges her to set out, 'the Princess was terribly flustered. Unsure of where she was headed, she felt helplessly forlorn' (Ch48, p.933); a lady-in-waiting rides with her, and 'the night was well advanced by the time she arrived at the Nijō villa' (Ch48, p.934). The three poems (48SW12–14) belong to this wrenching departure from Uji — leaving behind the river, the memory of her dead sister, and Kaoru. A transient site of transition between the Uji and capital worlds.
column3: En route from Uji to the capital (Naka no Kimi's move)

column1: En route from the Rokujō estate (the shared carriage)
column2: The road from the Rokujō estate, taken in a shared carriage by Yūgiri and Kashiwagi after the kickball match at which Kashiwagi glimpsed the Third Princess through a raised blind, in Wakana (Ch34, 'Early Herbs'). 'He and Genji's son rode away in the same carriage, and they talked along the way' (Ch34, p.618). Kashiwagi, secretly smitten, murmurs a veiled verse — 'Why does the warbler darting through blossoms / From tree to tree single out the cherry' (Ch34, p.618) — the cherry standing for the Third Princess; Yūgiri answers in kind. The two poems (34WJ21–22) are this carriage exchange, the first tremor of the affair that will bring Kashiwagi to ruin.
column3: En route from the Rokujō estate (the shared carriage)

column1: En route from the Sanjō residence to Nijō (dawn)
column2: The frosty dawn road by which Yūgiri travels from Princess Ōmiya's Sanjō residence back to his rooms at Genji's Nijō villa, in Otome (Ch21, 'Maidens of the Dance'). Devastated at being separated from Kumoinokari, he 'set off very early through the white frost of dawn to return to his rooms in the east annex at his father's Nijō villa,' the sky 'still dark and thickly overcast' (Ch21, p.391). The single poem (21OT06) is his grieving verse on the cold journey — 'Dark skies at dawn, when frost and ice...' A transient site of young heartbreak.
column3: En route from the Sanjō residence to Nijō (dawn)

column1: En route to Uji (Kaoru bringing Ukifune)
column2: The dawn road from the capital to Uji along which Kaoru carries Ukifune to install her at the Uji villa, in Azumaya (Ch50, 'A Hut in the Eastern Provinces'). Passing 'the banks of the Kamo River... in the vicinity of the Hōshōji Temple, it was daybreak' (Ch50, p.1034); as they climb deeper into the fog, Kaoru notices 'the blue of his robe stood out starkly against the scarlet of Ukifune's, creating an ominous impression of mourning robes dyed in futaai' (Ch50, p.1034) and murmurs a poem to himself. The single poem (50AM09) turns on this ill-omened color pairing — a subtle foreshadowing of grief. A transient site colored by Kaoru's unshakable mourning for Ōigimi.
column3: En route to Uji (Kaoru bringing Ukifune)

column1: En route to Uji (Kaoru's night journey)
column2: The fog-bound mountain road to Uji on Kaoru's first secret night visit to the Eighth Prince's villa, in Hashihime (Ch45, 'The Divine Princess at Uji Bridge'). 'Kaoru traveled on horseback; the closer he got to Uji, the denser the fog grew. As he made his way through undergrowth thick enough to obscure the path, he struggled against a fierce wind that shook the dew from the leaves of the trees' (Ch45, p.833), soaked and chilled, 'sad and exhilarated at the same time.' The single poem (45HS07) is his verse on the dew-drenched journey — the approach that opens the whole Uji drama.
column3: En route to Uji (Kaoru's night journey)

column2: Empress Fujitsubo's palace residence, the wisteria courtyard from which she takes her name: she was 'installed in the Higyōsha, which was also called the Fujitsubo because its chambers looked out onto a courtyard graced with wisteria' (Ch1, 'The Lady of the Paulownia-Courtyard Chambers,' p.15). In Momiji no ga (Ch7, 'An Imperial Celebration of Autumn Foliage') it stages the tale's most fraught tableau of concealed guilt: Genji 'performing music in Fujitsubo's quarters at the palace' while the Emperor joins them 'carrying the infant Prince in his arms' (Ch7, p.143) — the child secretly Genji's own. Fujitsubo's quarters are repeatedly figured as alluring precisely because they are 'off-limits' to Genji (cf. Ch8). The same physical courtyard reappears generations later as the Second Princess's quarters (see 'The Fujitsubo (the Second Princess's palace quarters),' Ch49).

Empress Fujitsubo's family home on Sanjō Avenue (the same site as 'Fujitsubo's residence,' Ch5, and 'Fujitsubo's family residence on Sanjō,' Ch10, 12), appearing here in Momiji no ga (Ch7, 'An Imperial Celebration of Autumn Foliage') as a locus of Genji's continuing, anguished attachment. The text does not describe the villa; see the Ch5 and Ch10 entries for the residence's fuller significance.

column1: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
column2: The palace chambers of Gen no Naishi — an Assistant Handmaid (Naishi no suke) 'nearing sixty' who, 'in spite of her age, tended to be outrageously flirtatious and utterly frivolous' (Momiji no ga, Ch7, 'An Imperial Celebration of Autumn Foliage,' p.146). Her chambers stage the chapter's comic subplot: Genji trades teasing notes and poems with her, and one rainy night, loitering near the Unmeiden, he hears her play the biwa 'in a most pleasing manner' and sing a saibara (Ch7, p.148). Tō no Chūjō, learning of the affair, takes it up as well and later bursts in on Genji during a tryst, the two friends scuffling in mock-heroic rivalry — a rare comic register in the palace's poetic geography.
column3: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace

column2: Genji's own villa at Katsura, on the far side of the river from the Akashi lady's Ōi residence, in Matsukaze (Ch18, 'Wind in the Pines'). Genji uses the pretext of 'business to attend to regarding a villa I'm building at Katsura' (Ch18, p.334) to visit Ōi. The villa is the setting of a splendid moonlit banquet: hearing 'the cries of the cormorant fishermen' recalls Suma to him (Ch18, p.339); noblemen returning from a hunt compose Chinese verses, and 'when the moon rose and was shining brightly, they began to play music in concert,' the 'soughing of the breeze off the river' an accompaniment (Ch18, p.339). Emperor Reizei, learning Genji is at Katsura, dispatches a Controller of the Chamberlain's office with a letter — 'I hear you stay across the river near the moon' (Ch18, p.339). The five poems (18xx) belong to this moon-viewing revelry.

The villa of the Governor of Kii, 'east of here in the vicinity of the Nakagawa River,' where 'he recently dammed the river up to make a stream and pond for his garden, so it's a cool, shaded place' (Hahakigi, Ch2, 'Broom Cypress,' p.38) — to which Genji repairs when a directional taboo blocks his return to the palace. Here Genji first encounters Utsusemi (the young wife of the Governor of Iyo, the Kii Governor's father), beginning the affair that runs through Utsusemi (Ch3, 'The Molted Cicada Shell'). The seven poems (02HH10–14, 03UT01–02) span the mansion's role as the setting of that famous episode. Cf. 'Nokiba no Ogi's marital home' (Ch4).

Hanachirusato's residence — 'the villa of the scattering orange blossoms' (Miotsukushi, Ch14, 'Channel Markers,' p.288) — in its Ch14 appearance, the same site as 'The Reikeiden Consort's villa' (Ch11) she shares with her sister. After his return from exile, Genji, 'preoccupied with his official responsibilities,' neglects her until 'the idle period of the rainy season,' when 'his longing for her stirred and he went to pay a visit' (Ch14, p.288). The two poems (14MT09–10) are their quiet reunion exchange — Hanachirusato having 'done nothing special to attract his attention,' the visit turns on Genji's constancy to an undemanding, gentle love rather than on the villa's appearance. Cf. 'The Reikeiden Consort's villa (Hanachirusato's quarters)' (Ch12).

The Hatsuse Kannon temple (Hasedera), at the base of Mount Miwa — 'one of the most famous temples in our land,' its reputation said to have 'spread even to China' (Tamakazura, Ch22, 'A Lovely Garland,' p.413), and among the most important pilgrimage sites of the age, its eleven-faced Kannon reputed especially efficacious in answering prayers (Ch22 translator's note). Tamakazura's party, newly returned from Kyūshū, makes the arduous journey here on foot to pray for her destiny, reaching 'the town of Tsubaichi at the base of Mount Miwa' after four days (Ch22, p.414). At the temple lodgings, by the working of Kannon, she is reunited with Ukon — her dead mother Yūgao's old attendant, who had prayed at Hasedera for exactly this — and the two exchange the 'twin cedars' verses ('Where two cedars stand, would I have met you,' Ch22, p.419), alluding to the sacred cedars of Mount Miwa. The pilgrimage reunion sets Tamakazura's whole story in motion. Hatsuse recurs as a pilgrimage destination through the Uji chapters.

The Major Captain Higekuro's mansion, the setting of the domestic tragedy at the heart of Makibashira (Ch31, 'A Beloved Pillar of Cypress'). When Higekuro takes Tamakazura as a second wife, his afflicted principal wife (Tō no Chūjō's daughter), seized by her malignant spirit, 'pulled out the censer from beneath the filigree basket used to perfume her husband's robes, walked up behind the Major Captain, and dumped the ashes on his head' (Ch31, p.521) — one of the tale's most startling scenes. As the marriage collapses and her father takes her home, her little daughter grieves to leave: at 'a pillar of cypress wood on the east side of her room' that was 'her favorite spot,' she writes a farewell poem on 'reddish-brown paper, a color similar to cypress bark' and thrusts it, with a hair-ornament, 'into a crack in the pillar' (Ch31, p.524) — the beloved cypress pillar that gives the chapter its title. The same site later centers on Tamakazura's household as Higekuro's widow (cf. 'Tamakazura's residence,' Ch44).

The family home of Genji's maternal grandmother, a widow, in Kiritsubo (Ch1, 'The Lady of the Paulownia-Courtyard Chambers') — the residence to which Genji's dying mother, the Kiritsubo Intimate, withdraws and where the infant Genji is kept. 'Although the grandmother of the little boy was a widow, she had carefully maintained appearances and made the residence a pleasant place to live in order to look after her only daughter' (Ch1, p.7), but after her daughter's death it falls under 'a dark mood.' The four poems (01KR02–05) are the grief-stricken exchange between the grandmother and the Emperor's messenger, Yugei no Myōbu, sent to inquire after the child.

column1: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
column2: A mountain temple 'in the recesses of the mountains north of the capital' (Wakamurasaki, Ch5, 'Little Purple Gromwell,' p.84), in the hills of Kitayama, where Genji goes to seek a healing ascetic for his recurring fever — and where he first discovers the child Murasaki. He arrives at cherry-blossom time: 'The cherry trees in the mountains... were still in full bloom, and as Genji's party wended its way deeper into the mountains, the cloudy mists rising at the peaks were breathtaking' (Ch5, p.84); the ascetic 'lived secluded amidst the crags of a high peak.' Below the temple stands the retreat of a certain bishop — 'a fence made of a wattle of branches... surrounded a pretty little house with a veranda situated amidst a fine stand of trees' (Ch5, p.85) — where the bishop's sister, a nun (Murasaki's grandmother), is convalescing. Peering 'through gaps in the fence' under cover of evening mist, Genji watches the nun at her devotions and glimpses the little girl who will become Murasaki (Ch5, p.87). The 15 poems here — the tale's densest cluster outside the great houses — include the 'young gromwell' (wakamurasaki) exchanges that give the chapter its name. The site's beauty and seclusion frame the fateful discovery.
column3: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama

column2: The residence of the fickle, koto-playing woman the Warden of the Left Mounted Guard was secretly seeing, in the rainy-night discussion of Hahakigi (Ch2, 'Broom Cypress') — who, he discovers, is entertaining another man. On an autumn night he watches a courtier serenade her, playing a flute among frost-touched chrysanthemums while 'her instrument reverberated clearly... a perfect accompaniment to the pure autumn moon' (Ch2, p.31), the man picking chrysanthemums and composing a poem (Ch2, p.31). The two poems (02HH03–04) belong to this moonlit scene of infidelity. Distinct from the finger-biting lady (also the Warden's); the setting exemplifies the untrustworthy, over-accomplished woman.

column1: House of the Finger Biting Lady
column2: The home of the fiercely jealous lady recalled by the Warden of the Left Mounted Guard in the rainy-night discussion of Hahakigi (Ch2, 'Broom Cypress'). When he feigned a break-up to cure her jealousy, 'the woman, unable to control her passions, grabbed my hand and bit one of my fingers' (Ch2, p.29); he stalked out with a poem, 'As I bend my wounded fingers counting.' The two poems (02HH01–02) are this finger-biting quarrel — a cautionary anecdote-setting rather than a described place, exemplifying, in the discussion's taxonomy, the unbearably jealous wife.
column3: House of the Finger Biting Lady

column1: Kaoru's Sanjō residence (the Third Princess's, rebuilt)
column2: Kaoru's residence — the Third Princess's Sanjō mansion, rebuilt after the fire that destroyed her earlier Sanjō villa (Ch47), to which Kaoru moves from Rokujō in Sawarabi (Ch48, 'Bracken Shoots'). It stands close to Genji's old Nijō villa — near enough that in spring 'Kaoru gazed across from his new residence on Sanjō toward the blossoms at the Nijō villa' (Ch48, p.935), the sight carrying his thoughts back to the deserted house at Uji. His mother the Third Princess and her women live here, and his wife the Second Princess (the 'Fujitsubo Princess') moves in after their marriage (Yadorigi, Ch49). As a poetic site it is understated — the narrative dwells on Kaoru's restless interiority rather than on the house — but it is the point from which his letters to Ukifune at Uji originate and where he reads her replies (Ukifune, Ch51), where his grief-poems and the picture exchange with the First Princess center (Kagerō, Ch52), and from which he sends the tale's final letter-poem to Ukifune at Ono (Yume no ukihashi, Ch54). Distinct from the older Sanjō residences of Ōmiya and Yūgiri and from the small Sanjō hideaway of Ch50.
column3: Kaoru's Sanjō residence (the Third Princess's, rebuilt)

column2: The Gojō residence of Genji's old wet-nurse (Koremitsu's mother), 'the wife of the Senior Assistant Governor General for Kyūshū,' now gravely ill and newly a nun, whom Genji stops to visit in Yūgao (Ch4, 'The Lady of the Evening Faces'). Finding 'the gate where he usually entered was bolted shut,' he waits while Koremitsu is summoned (Ch4, p.55) — and it is next door to this house that he notices the evening-faces vine. Genji consoles the dying nurse, urging her to 'live a long life so that you'll be able to watch me rise through the ranks.' The two poems (04xx) are their tender parting exchange — the doorstep from which the whole Yūgao episode opens. Cf. 'Yūgao's House on Gojō.'

The residence of Koremitsu (Genji's foster-brother and retainer, now Governor of Settsu), where his daughter — whom Genji presents as a Gosechi dancer — returns after the festival, in Otome (Ch21, 'Maidens of the Dance'). The smitten young Yūgiri sends the girl a covert love-poem here; she is 'enchanted by the note,' which was 'written on thin green paper that matched the color of the coral evergreen garland she had worn for the Gosechi dance,' and 'stylishly bundled with other sheets of various colors' (Ch21, p.395). As she and her brother read it, 'Koremitsu suddenly entered' and, startling them, 'took it from them' (Ch21, p.395). The single poem (21OT10) is Yūgiri's first love-letter — a notable instance of paper chosen to match its occasion.

The villa of Kōbai (Tō no Chūjō's second son, Kashiwagi's brother), in Kōbai (Ch43, 'Red Plum'). To house his three daughters at their coming-of-age, 'Kōbai widened the main hall of his villa to eight pillars to create seven openings along the front facade' (Ch43, p.792) — a mark of high rank (a translator's note observes that seven openings exceeded the usual six-pillar span). Each daughter is given a quarter facing a different direction. The residence's signature image is 'a lovely red plum tree blooming near the eaves on the east side of the main hall' whose 'enchanting fragrance' (Ch43, p.795) prompts Kōbai to break off a branch and send it to Niou at the palace — a courtship overture trading on the Prince's own celebrated scent. Stands just to the east of Tamakazura's residence (Ch44).

The refined Sixth-Avenue residence of the Rokujō lady (the Rokujō Haven), Genji's proud, older, cultivated mistress — the site later absorbed, after her death, into Genji's grand Rokujō estate. It appears first in Yūgao (Ch4, 'Lady of the Evening Faces'), where Genji's ardor is already cooling: after 'one of his now infrequent visits to Rokujō, a heavy fog blanketed the surroundings' (Ch4, p.61), and he lingers over 'the variegated autumn foliage of the garden' as an elegant lady-in-waiting sees him off at dawn. The residence's atmosphere is autumnal, exquisite, and faintly melancholy — a mirror of its mistress's wounded refinement. The same site recurs as 'Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)' (Ch9) and 'The Rokujō villa (Lady Rokujō's residence)' (Ch14); it is likely the land later incorporated into 'The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)' (Ch21+).

Tamino Island at Naniwa, where the Akashi lady undergoes her purification rite, in Miotsukushi (Ch14, 'Channel Markers'). 'She attached her poem to one of the cords of paper mulberry bark used during the Purification Ritual she underwent on Tamino Island and sent it to Genji' (Ch14, p.293) — the verse playing on the island's name (Tamino, 'isle of straw raincoats'): 'Even the name Tamino, isle of straw raincoats' (Ch14, p.293). The single poem is delivered by messenger, attached to a ritual mulberry-bark streamer — a notable instance of a poem's physical medium being drawn from the sacred rite itself.

The Akashi lady's boat at Naniwa, in Miotsukushi (Ch14, 'Channel Markers'). Having sailed to Sumiyoshi for her annual pilgrimage only to find the shore overwhelmed by Genji's grand procession, and 'overcome with an unpleasant sense of inferiority,' she resolves, 'I'll have my boat put in at Naniwa and undergo purification there,' and 'had her men row the boat away' (Ch14, p.291). The single poem belongs to this humiliated withdrawal by water — the Akashi lady's boat a recurring emblem of her lowly, drifting station beside Genji's splendor. One of several closely linked Naniwa water-sites in this chapter.

The Horie canal at Naniwa, 'famous in poetry for the poles used to mark out the boat channels,' viewed by Genji on his return tour after the Sumiyoshi pilgrimage, in Miotsukushi (Ch14, 'Channel Markers'). Genji 'instinctively murmured a line of verse: "Now, in this very city of Naniwa"' (Ch14, p.292); Koremitsu, ever ready, produces 'the short-handled brush and paper' he keeps in his robe, and Genji writes the channel-marker (miotsukushi) verse, 'Like the channel markers leading us here,' that gives the chapter its title (Ch14, p.292). The single poem turns on the pun of miotsukushi — channel-marker and 'exhausting oneself' — as a pledge to the Akashi lady.

The Naniwa inlet at evening, in Miotsukushi (Ch14, 'Channel Markers'), the setting for the exchange between Genji and the Akashi lady after their near-miss at Sumiyoshi. Her reply reaches him at sunset: 'The evening tide was rising and cranes, with their lovely cries, were calling out from the inlet,' the poignant scene making Genji long 'to meet her regardless of who might see them' (Ch14, p.293). The two poems belong to this tidal, crane-haunted twilight. Closely linked to the other Naniwa water-sites of this chapter (the boat, the Horie canal, Tamino Island).

Genji's private villa on Nijō (Second Avenue), his principal home for most of the tale and second only to Rokujō in poetic output (116 poems, Ch3–52). Originally the residence of Genji's late mother, it is rebuilt and enlarged for him in Kiritsubo (Ch1, 'The Lady of the Paulownia-Courtyard Chambers'): the Office of Palace Repairs is ordered to 'rebuild and expand the former residence of Genji's mother, a villa on Nijō Avenue,' so splendidly that 'there would be no other villa like it,' its woods and hills already 'unparalleled' and its enlarged garden pond so eye-catching that 'it created a stir' (Ch1, p.19). Genji imagines it as the perfect home for a wife like Fujitsubo (Ch1, p.19) — a wish half-realized when, in Wakamurasaki (Ch5, 'Little Purple Gromwell'), he carries the child Murasaki here before dawn to the west hall, which 'was not usually inhabited, and there were no curtains or furnishings' until he has it furnished from the east hall (Ch5, p.111). Nijō is thus above all Murasaki's home, organized around her west hall and the east annex added for Hanachirusato; Murasaki dies here in Minori (Ch40). After Genji's death Niou treats Nijō as his own residence (Ch42), installs Naka no Kimi in the west hall (Sawarabi, Ch48) where she bears his son (Yadorigi, Ch49); here Chūjō no Kimi shelters Ukifune and Niou intrudes on her (Azumaya, Ch50), and Niou intercepts Ukifune's New Year letter and sends his rain-letters from his sickbed (Ukifune, Ch51). Poems flow to and from Nijō across three generations — Genji, Murasaki, Hanachirusato, Niou, Naka no Kimi, Kaoru — making it the connective hub of the capital's poetic correspondence.

The marital home of Nokiba no Ogi — Utsusemi's stepdaughter, with whom Genji had 'mistakenly slept that night' (Yūgao, Ch4, 'The Lady of the Evening Faces') during his pursuit of Utsusemi — after she is 'betrothed to a Lesser Captain in the Palace Guard' (Ch4, p.81). Curious about her, Genji sends her a message at her new home (Ch4, p.81). The two poems (04xx) are this after-the-fact exchange with the young wife he took by error. The residence is undescribed; its interest lies in the wry aftermath of the mistaken tryst.

column1: On the Uji River (the boat to the Isle of Tachibana)
column2: The Uji River itself, as the setting of the title scene of Ukifune (Ch51, 'A Boat Cast Adrift'). To spirit Ukifune away for a secret tryst, Niou takes her by boat across the water: 'They boarded one of those small boats that she had gazed out on every day... As they were crossing the river, she felt isolated and lonely, as if she were being separated from everything, rowing away toward some remote, far-off shore' (Ch51, p.1059), while 'the twentieth-day moon rose clear and high, its beams reflecting off the pellucid surface of the water.' The oarsman draws up at 'the Isle of Tachibana,' 'shaped like an enormous rock,' its evergreens casting 'deep shadows' (Ch51, p.1059). Here the lovers exchange the ukifune ('a boat cast adrift') verses that name the chapter and crystallize Ukifune's sense of herself as rudderless — the image that foreshadows her attempt to drown in this same river.
column3: On the Uji River (the boat to the Isle of Tachibana)

column1: On the journey to Suma (the ruined Ōe villa, Yodo River)
column2: A ruined villa on the banks of the Yodo River, glimpsed by Genji as he journeys into exile in Suma (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'). Having boarded a boat at the Fushimi landing, he 'viewed the site of the Ōe villa on the banks of the Yodo River. Once used as a lodge for the Ise Priestess on her return to the capital, it was now in utter ruins, with only pine trees remaining as a marker of where the buildings once stood' (Ch12, p.236). The single poem (12xx) is Genji's meditation on the ruin — an emblem of transience and lost glory mirroring his own fall. A transient waypoint, described only in passing.
column3: On the journey to Suma (the ruined Ōe villa, Yodo River)

column1: Outside the Uji villa (Niou's failed night visit)
column2: The approach to the Uji villa, where Niou is turned away on a later clandestine visit in Ukifune (Ch51, 'A Boat Cast Adrift'). By now Kaoru has grown suspicious and posted vigilant watchmen, so that Niou, arriving in disguise, cannot be received: he is left 'waiting on horseback a short distance away' when 'wild-sounding dogs came rushing out,' their 'ferocious growling and howling' terrifying his small, nervous escort (Ch51, p.1080). Unable to reach Ukifune, he shelters 'beneath the shade of a weed-tangled hedge enclosing a peasant's hut,' where Tokikata spreads a saddle blanket and helps him dismount (Ch51, p.1080). His frustrated soliloquy (51UF18) is composed here, outside the barred villa — the scene marking the collapse of his access to Ukifune and hastening the crisis that drives her toward the river.
column3: Outside the Uji villa (Niou's failed night visit)

column2: An earlier reference to Princess Asagao's home, in Aoi (Ch9, 'Leaves of Wild Ginger'), where Genji — relying as ever on her lady-in-waiting Chūjō as go-between — continues his long, unrequited correspondence with the Bellflower Princess. The residence is not described here; it is chiefly a destination for Genji's persistent letters. The same lady's fuller home appears as 'The Momozono estate (Princess Asagao's residence)' (Ch20–32), her father Prince Shikibu's estate north of Ichijō.

Genji's place of self-imposed exile on the Suma coast (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'; also Ch13) — in antiquity 'a site where the nobility had built villas and estates,' but by Genji's day 'a desolate, deserted backwater dotted with a scattering of fishermen's huts' (Ch12, p.225), 'those desolate shores where there would be no callers except for the wind and the waves' (Ch12, p.225). Genji brings only essentials — 'a box containing essential books, including the collected poems of Bai Juyi and... a seven-string koto' — resolving to 'adopt the appearance of a poor mountain peasant' (Ch12, p.231). His abode stands near where the exiled poet Ariwara no Yukihira once grieved: 'situated on a disturbingly isolated hill a short distance in from the shore. Everything about the place, starting with the fence, seemed a marvel to him. The thatched roofs of the small pavilions and the reed-thatched roofs of what were apparently the passageways connecting the buildings were pleasantly elegant' (Ch12, p.237). Suma is the tale's great landscape of loneliness and exile-poetry — the salt-makers' fires, the smoke, the sea-wind, the terrifying storm — and generates 30 poems, the most of any exile site. Genji is delivered from it when the Akashi Priest's boat carries him on to Akashi (Ch13).

The great shrine at Sumiyoshi (near Naniwa), dedicated to the god of the sea and of poetry, and a focus of thanksgiving pilgrimage. Its central scene is in Miotsukushi (Ch14, 'Channel Markers'): 'In the autumn, Genji went on a pilgrimage to the Sumiyoshi Shrine' with 'a magnificent procession in order to offer thanks to the deity there for granting his prayers' (Ch14, p.290). By coincidence the Akashi lady arrives by boat the same day on her own annual pilgrimage; seeing 'the beach packed with bustling, noisy pilgrims' and, 'against the dark green backdrop of the fields of pine trees,' the courtiers' robes like 'the scattering blossoms of spring or the foliage of fall' (Ch14, p.291), she is 'overcome with an unpleasant sense of inferiority' (Ch14, p.291) and has her boat rowed away to Naniwa without landing (Ch14, p.292). The channel-marker (miotsukushi) verses they exchange give the chapter its title. Genji returns for a second grand thanksgiving pilgrimage, with Murasaki and the Akashi entourage, in Wakana ge (Ch35). The shrine embodies the working of divine favor over the Akashi destiny.

The mountain temple that Retired Emperor Suzaku builds as his religious retreat, 'in the hills west of the capital' (Wakana, Ch34, 'Early Herbs,' p.566), and to which he withdraws after taking the tonsure. From it he cannot stop worrying over the Third Princess, whom he has entrusted to Genji. Its poetic moment comes in Yokobue (Ch37, 'The Transverse Flute'): 'Retired Emperor Suzaku, off in his mountain temple' (Ch37, p.696), gathers rustic gifts of the season — 'The bamboo shoots emerging in the nearby forest and the taro roots growing in the vicinity of his temple struck Suzaku as touching symbols of the rustic life of a mountain village' — and sends them to the Third Princess with a letter (Ch37, p.697). The accompanying verses turn on the bamboo shoots and her little son. Distinct from Suzaku's pre-retirement residence (see 'The Suzaku Palace').

Tamakazura's mansion as the widow of the late Chancellor (Higekuro), 'close to the Third Princess's villa on Sanjō' (Takekawa, Ch44, 'Bamboo River,' p.801), and the principal setting of that chapter (20 poems). After the Chancellor's death 'his bustling residence grew deserted and quiet' (Ch44, p.800); the household now revolves around Tamakazura's two marriageable daughters and their suitors, and the young Kaoru, whom she treats as a younger brother, visits often. Its loveliest scene comes in the third month, when 'the cherry trees were in full flower and clouds of scattering blossoms filled the air' (Ch44, p.807): the two sisters, seated at a Go board out near the veranda, wager the garden cherry tree 'that they had been arguing over ever since they were young girls' — 'Whoever wins two out of three matches may claim the tree' (Ch44, p.808) — while their attendants form rival cheering sides and a suitor watches, smitten, from the mist. Likely the same site as 'Higekuro's residence' (Ch31).

column1: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
column2: The Akashi lady's separate dwelling, 'at the foot of the hills' on her father's Akashi estate, to which the Priest moved her for safety and where Genji courts her, in Akashi (Ch13, 'The Lady at Akashi'). Genji rides out to it by moonlight on the thirteenth of the eighth month, finding 'the villa at the foot of the hill was farther away than he thought' (Ch13, p.269). It is a place of exquisite, solitary melancholy: 'The villa of the Akashi lady, a stylish structure with many admirable touches, was set deep in a grove of trees. Whereas the residence near the shore that Genji was using was grand and attractive, the villa here was the kind of place where a person would lead a forlorn, solitary existence' (Ch13, p.269), 'the roots of the pine trees growing on the craggy rocks... a tasteful backdrop,' insects filling the garden, the meditation-hall bell 'mingling with the soughing of the pines' (Ch13, p.269). Here Genji and the Akashi lady begin their affair — their daughter, the future Empress, is conceived here — and the salt-fire and autumn-wave imagery colors their exchanges (Ch13, p.274). With 15 poems, it is the richest Akashi site. Cf. 'Akashi (the Akashi Lady's residence)' (Ch14).
column3: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)

column2: The seaside villa on the Akashi Priest's estate that Genji occupies after the storm drives him from Suma, in Akashi (Ch13, 'The Lady at Akashi'). The Priest (a novitiate, the former Governor of Harima) had built along the shore 'a thatched-roof cottage near the shore that would intensify the pleasures of viewing the four seasons,' with 'a magnificent meditation hall standing beside a stream flowing down out of the hills' and 'a row of granaries' (Ch13, p.259); fearing tidal surges, he moved his daughter inland and gave Genji 'the villa near the sea' (Ch13, p.259). It far outshines the Suma villa: 'much brighter and more cheerful,' its elegance 'no different from that enjoyed at the most distinguished aristocratic houses in the capital' (Ch13, p.259). The setting defies painting — 'the elegance of the buildings, the indescribable appearance of the grove of trees... the rocks and plants in the gardens, the waters of the inlet' (Ch13, p.259). Distinct from the Akashi lady's hillside villa (see 'The Akashi Lady's villa'). Cf. 'Akashi (the Akashi Priest's seaside residence)' for its later appearances.

The home of the learned scholar's daughter recalled by the Junior Secretary of the Ministry of Rites in the rainy-night discussion of Hahakigi (Ch2, 'Broom Cypress'). Erudite and 'almost mannish' in her writing, she once put him off with a comically pedantic excuse: 'These past few months I have been indisposed by a severe malady and prescribed a regimen of herbal tonic concocted mainly of garlic. This has rendered me extremely malodorous' (Ch2, p.35), and bade him return 'when this odor has dissipated.' The two poems (02HH08–09) are the resulting garlic-scented exchange (Ch2, p.36). A satirical anecdote-setting mocking the over-educated woman.

column1: The Crown Prince's quarters at the palace
column2: The palace quarters of the Crown Prince (the future Emperor Reizei, secretly Genji's son), tended by the gentlewoman Ōmyōbu. In Suma (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'), on the point of exile and 'unable to visit you again before I leave,' Genji sends the boy a farewell letter and poem 'attached to a branch of cherry from which the petals had already dropped' (Ch12, p.234); the child-Prince maintains 'a serious countenance' as he reads it (Ch12). The site appears only here (2 poems); its poignancy lies in the correspondence, not in any description of the place.
column3: The Crown Prince's quarters at the palace

column1: The Fujitsubo (the Second Princess's palace quarters)
column2: The same wisteria courtyard (the Higyōsha) as Empress Fujitsubo's early residence, here the quarters of the reigning Emperor's Second Princess — whom the translator calls the 'Fujitsubo Princess' from her association with these chambers — whom Kaoru weds. In Yadorigi (Ch49, 'Trees Encoiled in Vines of Ivy'), on the day before she moves to Kaoru's Sanjō, 'His Majesty went over to his daughter's residence in the Fujitsubo to hold a banquet celebrating the wisteria blooms there,' the blinds of the south aisle raised and 'the imperial throne set up facing the courtyard' (Ch49, p.987). The wisteria-banquet verses (49YD20–23) are composed here; Kaoru 'descended into the courtyard to fetch a spray of wisteria blossoms to adorn His Majesty's cap' (Ch49, p.987) before intoning his own. Same physical chambers as 'Fujitsubo's palace residence (the Higyōsha).'
column3: The Fujitsubo (the Second Princess's palace quarters)

column2: The river-barge of the former Gosechi dancer (an old flame of Genji's), passing the Suma coast in Suma (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'). She 'contrived to get a note off to Genji' with a verse likening her feelings to 'the towrope on this barge' (Ch12, p.246); Genji replies, teasing whether she can 'simply drift past these shoals at Suma.' 'Having received such an elegant reply, the Gosechi dancer was thrilled... She felt as if she ought to disembark and stay behind at Suma' (Ch12, p.246). The two poems (12xx) are this passing shipboard exchange — a fleeting, poignant contact with the capital world Genji has left behind.

The estate of the late Prince Hitachi, home to his daughter the Safflower Princess (Suetsumuhana), whom he 'raised with considerable care' but left 'in rather dire circumstances' after his death (Suetsumuhana, Ch6, 'The Safflower,' p.114). Genji, hearing of the lonely Princess from the go-between Taifu, resolves to 'sneak over to the Hitachi villa on an evening when the moon is obscured by mist' (Ch6, p.115). The residence is defined by its 'strange, mournful atmosphere' (Ch6, p.118) — a decaying aristocratic house where a timid, impoverished Princess lives among a dwindling household. It is here Genji hears her koto and, in the celebrated morning-after scene, at last glimpses her unfortunate long red nose. The same estate reappears, further ruined, in Yomogiu (Ch15) — see 'The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate).'

The same estate of the late Prince Hitachi (see 'The Hitachi villa,' Ch6), now fallen into spectacular ruin, in Yomogiu (Ch15, 'A Ruined Villa of Tangled Gardens') — one of the tale's great set-pieces of decay. After the Prince's death the villa 'became a haven for foxes and a place where owls could be heard day and night in the eerily deserted groves' (Ch15, p.300); its gardens grew so overgrown 'that the ground had disappeared beneath the wild profusion of satin-tail grass and mugwort, which had grown so tall that they seemed to be vying with each other to reach the eaves' (Ch15, p.301), the weeds choking the gates east and west. The great storm that struck Genji at Suma 'blew down sections of the galleries' (Ch15, p.301). Yet the loyal Safflower Princess refuses to give up 'the last vestiges of my father's estate' (Ch15, p.300), and it is here, pushing through the weeds after his return from exile, that Genji rediscovers and rescues her.

column1: The Ichijō villa (the Second Princess's residence)
column2: The residence of the Second Princess (Ochiba), Kashiwagi's widow, where she lives with her mother the Miyasudokoro — the setting of Yūgiri's slow, mist-shrouded courtship in Yūgiri (Ch39, 'Evening Mist') and a recurring locus from the Kashiwagi chapters (Ch35–38; 13 poems). Genji's son, 'a serious and learned gentleman,' visits under 'the pretense that his visits were motivated by his remembrance of the past and his promises to Kashiwagi,' his love growing 'as the days and months went by' (Ch39, p.716). When the mother sickens and retreats to a mountain villa in Ono, Yūgiri follows; after her death he installs the Second Princess back at this residence and presses the marriage. Note: the traditional place-name 'Ichijō' does not appear in Washburn's translation, which calls it simply the Second Princess's residence; the villa itself is given little physical description, the narrative's attention falling on the autumn Ono retreat and the emotional siege of the courtship.
column3: The Ichijō villa (the Second Princess's residence)

column2: The Ise Shrine, where the Rokujō lady has accompanied her daughter the High Priestess, and from which she corresponds with the exiled Genji at Suma in Suma (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'). The narrator marks the exchange of messengers: 'Genji had sent a messenger to the Ise Shrine with a letter for his Rokujō lady, and she had in turn... dispatched her own messenger to inquire after him,' her letter's 'words and... calligraphy... exceptionally refined' (Ch12, p.240). She laments that 'by remaining here among the deities at this Shinto shrine, I am being deeply sinful in the eyes of the Buddha' (Ch12, p.240) — the tension between Shinto service and Buddhist salvation. The four poems (12SM25–28) are their Ise–Suma exchange; Genji keeps the Priestess's young messenger on for several days, so touched is he. The shrine is present only through this correspondence; the text gives its religious situation, not its appearance.

column1: The Ise departure procession (passing Genji's Nijō villa)
column2: The point on the Rokujō lady's departure route for Ise where her procession passes Genji's Nijō villa, in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). 'The party set off after dark, and when they turned at Nijō Avenue onto the boulevard of Tōin, they passed by Genji's villa. Feeling great pity, Genji attached a poem to a branch of sakaki and sent it to the lady' (Ch10, p.197). The single poem (10SK08) is his roadside farewell verse — notable for its delivery by messenger, tied to a sacred-evergreen branch, to a moving procession. A transient site defined by parting.
column3: The Ise departure procession (passing Genji's Nijō villa)

column2: The festival-viewing grounds along Ichijō Avenue during the Kamo Festival, in Aoi (Ch9, 'Leaves of Wild Ginger'). For the new High Priestess's inaugural procession, 'the thoroughfare of Ichijō was crammed with carriages and bustling with people. Viewing platforms had been erected at various sites and decorated with great care' (Ch9, p.163). It is out of such festival viewing that the notorious carriage quarrel between Aoi and the Rokujō lady erupts (see 'The lustration procession'). Genji, invited to watch the parade from a stranger's well-placed carriage — 'an excellent place from which to view the festival parade' (Ch9, p.167) — trades flirtatious verses with its coquettish occupant. The three poems (09AO04–06) belong to this festival-day flirtation.

The Kamo Festival procession as viewed by Murasaki in Fuji no uraba (Ch33, 'Shoots of Wisteria Leaves'). Having attended the Miare Rites the night before, 'Murasaki prayed at the shrine at dawn, then stopped at a viewing stand on the way back to the Rokujō estate to watch the procession escorting the High Priestess to the Kamo River for the Purification Ritual' (Ch33, p.558). The other Rokujō ladies' carriages draw up in a line before her stand, and 'even from a distance onlookers knew very well it was Murasaki who was seated there' — a serene, splendid tableau that pointedly recalls, and reverses, the humiliating carriage quarrel of Ch9. The two poems (33xx) attach to this viewing.

The Kamo Shrine, where Princess Asagao serves as High Priestess after her father Prince Shikibu's death, in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). While on retreat at the nearby Urin'in, Genji, who has never abandoned his suit, sends her a letter: 'The Kamo Shrine was close enough to the Urin'in that a breeze might have been able to carry a message, so Genji sent a letter off to the Priestess there, Princess Asagao' (Ch10, p.207), by way of her attendant Chūjō. The two poems (10xx) are their guarded exchange across the sacred precinct — the shrine's ritual purity heightening the impropriety of his courtship. Cf. 'The Urin'in Temple.'

A residential courtyard in the imperial palace, named for its paulownia trees (kiri), and the chambers of Genji's mother, the Kiritsubo Intimate. Kiritsubo (Ch1, 'The Lady of the Paulownia-Courtyard Chambers') fixes its geography and its pathos: 'Her chambers at the palace were in the Kiritsubo—named for its courtyard, which was graced with paulownia trees,' set 'in the northeast corner of the palace, and thus separated from the Emperor's quarters in the Seiryōden' (Ch1, p.4), so that the Emperor's constant visits, which oblige him to pass the other ladies' chambers, provoke the jealousy that destroys her. After his mother's death Genji makes the Kiritsubo his own palace base — 'when Genji stayed at the palace, he took up residence in the Kiritsubo' (Ch1, p.19) — and it lodges him through the early chapters (31 poems, Ch2–8), the point from which many of his youthful love-poems issue. The text foregrounds its emotional charge over its architecture; its recurring image is the lonely paulownia courtyard the grieving Emperor cannot forget.

A major palace courtyard, residence of the Kokiden Consort — Genji's political enemy and mother of the Crown Prince. Its dramatic poetic moment comes in Hana no en (Ch8, 'A Banquet Celebrating Cherry Blossoms'): after the cherry banquet Genji, finding Fujitsubo's door barred, 'moved down the narrow passageway toward the Kokiden and found the third door open,' the chambers 'deserted' (Ch8, p.155), and overhears a young woman softly reciting 'Nothing compares to this spring evening of a misty moon' (Ch8, p.155). This is Oborozukiyo; the two pass the night and at dawn 'hastily exchanged fans as a token of their tryst' (Ch8, p.156) without her ever giving her name — one of the tale's most consequential chance encounters, launching the affair that helps send Genji into exile. Genji privately finds the Kokiden less stylish than Fujitsubo's off-limits quarters (Ch8).

column1: The Kujō lodging (Tamakazura's residence in the capital)
column2: The dreary lodging at Kujō where Tamakazura's party first shelters on reaching the capital after fleeing Kyūshū, in Tamakazura (Ch22, 'A Lovely Garland'). 'Calling on an old acquaintance who was living at Kujō, they were able to find lodging at his residence. Although the place was inside the city proper, it was not an area where the most upright, reliable folks lived, and with all the women peddlers and merchants plying their trade there, they found it dreary and not at all' welcoming (Ch22, p.412). The two poems (22xx) belong to this bleak, temporary refuge, from which the nurse resolves to seek divine help at Hatsuse. The lodging embodies the family's precarious, displaced state before Tamakazura is restored to the aristocratic world.
column3: The Kujō lodging (Tamakazura's residence in the capital)

column2: A hall of the palace 'next to the Seiryōden' (Ch1, 'The Lady of the Paulownia-Courtyard Chambers,' p.5). In Kiritsubo the Emperor, pitying his ailing favorite the long walk from the distant Kiritsubo, 'ordered that she be installed in the chambers of the Kōrōden,' displacing 'the lady who had resided there from the very beginning,' who is left to 'nurse a deep resentment' (Ch1, p.5) — an early instance of the palace's geography of rank and grievance. The site appears only here (1 poem); the text gives its position and political meaning rather than its appearance.

The capital home of the late Major Counselor — Murasaki's maternal grandfather — whose widow, now a nun, nurses the orphaned child here, in Wakamurasaki (Ch5, 'Little Purple Gromwell'). The Kitayama Bishop explains: 'his primary wife is my younger sister. After he died, she took religious vows and left her household to become a nun' (Ch5, p.90). After the grandmother returns from Kitayama and dies, the little girl is left here — close to Genji's Nijō villa — and Genji, before dawn, carries her off to Nijō (Ch5). The residence is scarcely described; it functions as the threshold from which Murasaki passes into Genji's keeping.

column1: The Minister of the Right's residence
column2: The Nijō mansion of the Minister of the Right, father of the Kokiden Consort and of Oborozukiyo — the stronghold of Genji's political adversaries. Newly 'renovated and decorated lavishly' for his granddaughters' coming-of-age, its 'ornate, gaudy style . . . reflected his personal taste, and everything had a modern, up-to-date air' (Hana no en, Ch8, 'A Banquet Celebrating Cherry Blossoms,' p.158). Its great scene is the archery-and-wisteria banquet just after the twentieth of the third month (Ch8, p.158), where Genji — reluctantly attending — re-encounters Oborozukiyo, deepening the perilous affair begun at the palace. The residence returns in Wakana (Ch34–35), where the widowed Oborozukiyo withdraws and eventually takes vows. Distinct from Tō no Chūjō's Nijō residence.
column3: The Minister of the Right's residence

column1: The Minister's Uji estate (Yūgiri's, across the river)
column2: Yūgiri's country estate on the far bank of the Uji River, opposite the Eighth Prince's villa: 'Genji's son, the Minister, had inherited from his father a handsome, expansive estate away from the capital on the far side of the Uji River' (Shiigamoto, Ch46, 'At the Foot of the Oak Tree,' p.847). When Niou breaks his Hatsuse pilgrimage to stop at Uji, the estate is 'prepared for the guests in a most delightful fashion,' with 'boards for Go, backgammon,' and an evening concert (Ch46, p.847). It stands in pointed contrast to the austere villa across the water: Kaoru and his companions 'punted across the river' to call on Hachinomiya, whose simple dwelling 'was very different from the residence across the river' (Ch46, p.848). A translator's note flags a source inconsistency — Genji's son, promoted to Minister of the Left in Ch44, is variously called 'the Minister of the Right' and simply 'the Minister' through the Uji chapters. Distinct from the humble Tachibana hideaway of Ch51 (see 'The house across the Uji River').
column3: The Minister's Uji estate (Yūgiri's, across the river)

column1: The Ono villa (the mountain retreat near Mt. Hiei)
column2: A temporary mountain villa at Ono, 'near the foot of Mount Hiei' (Yūgiri, Ch39, 'Evening Mist,' p.716), to which the Second Princess (Ochiba) and her mother the Miyasudokoro retreat when the mother, 'assailed by a malignant spirit,' seeks the healing rites of a master priest who had vowed never to leave the mountain (Ch39, p.716). The villa is modest but refined: 'The small fence of fine wattle that surrounded the Ono villa was elegantly constructed, and though the residence was only for temporary use, it had a polished feel to it,' with an earthwork fire-altar on the east, the Second Princess lodged on the west and her mother in the north aisle rooms (Ch39, p.717). Its setting yields one of the tale's loveliest evening landscapes: 'The sun was beginning to set, and a lovely mist poignantly veiled the sky... The sound of flowing water was cool and refreshing. The wind blowing down the mountains made one feel a sublime loneliness. The soughing of the wind echoed deeply through the pine forests' (Ch39, p.719), as a bell rings and monks chant sutras in relay. It is here that Yūgiri, having escorted the ailing pair to Ono, presses his long-restrained suit on the Second Princess; the evening mists veiling the wattle fence give both his poem and the chapter their name (Ch39, p.719). The mother, learning of his advances, dies of shock and grief soon after. Distinct from the nuns' residence at Ono of Ch53–54.
column3: The Ono villa (the mountain retreat near Mt. Hiei)

column2: A bare, generic reference to the imperial palace, carrying a single poem in Momiji no ga (Ch7, 'An Imperial Celebration of Autumn Foliage,' 07MM17). The label marks a scene set at court without the text specifying a particular hall or courtyard; nothing is said of the place itself. Cf. the many named palace halls and the qualified 'The palace (...)' scene-labels.

The quiet villa of the former Reikeiden Consort (one of Genji's late father's imperial consorts) and her younger sister Hanachirusato, the Lady of the Scattering Orange Blossoms, in Hanachirusato (Ch11, 'The Lady at the Villa of Scattering Orange Blossoms'). Genji visits on the twentieth of the fifth month, drawn by a cuckoo's cry, and finds it 'just the way he had imagined: quiet, nearly deserted, with no sign of any visitors' (Ch11, p.224). 'The lovely fragrance of a mandarin orange tree under the nearby eaves wafted in, reminding Genji and the Consort of people from their pasts' (Ch11, p.224), and a cuckoo calls in the dark garden — the orange blossoms and cuckoo that give the chapter its imagery. The villa is a haven of faded elegance and gentle constancy. Cf. 'The Reikeiden Consort's villa (Hanachirusato's quarters)' (Ch12).

column1: The Reizei villa (Retired Emperor Reizei's residence)
column2: The retirement residence of Retired Emperor Reizei (secretly Genji's son), where he lives with the Umetsubo Empress (Akikonomu) after abdicating 'of his own volition at the very height of a glorious reign' (Suzumushi, Ch38, 'Bell Crickets,' p.713). Its warmest scene is Genji's impromptu moonlit visit in that chapter: leaving the bell-cricket gathering at Rokujō, he leads an informal procession — flutes played 'casually' beneath a late-night sky — 'to the residence of the Retired Emperor' (Ch38, p.713), where 'many poems, both in Chinese and Japanese, were composed that evening' until dawn, Reizei 'surprised and utterly delighted by his arrival' and Genji 'deeply touched to see how peaceful he was in retirement' (Ch38). The villa recurs (Ch40, 44, 45) as the tranquil seat of Reizei's cultivated retirement. Distinct from 'The palace (Emperor Reizei's residence)' (Ch18), his quarters while reigning.
column3: The Reizei villa (Retired Emperor Reizei's residence)

column1: The Retired Emperor's tomb (hills north of the capital)
column2: The grave of Genji's late father, the Retired Emperor, in the hills north of the capital, which Genji visits by night to take leave before his exile in Suma (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'). 'The path leading up to the grave was overgrown with grasses, and the dew further soaked Genji's robes, which were already damp with his tears'; 'clouds obscured the moon, and the dark shadows of the dense forest were oppressive and unsettling,' and as he prayed 'a vision of his father's face, looking as if he were alive, appeared clearly in front of him, sending an involuntary chill through his body' (Ch12, p.234). The single poem (12xx) is Genji's graveside address to his father's silent spirit — one of the tale's eeriest, most desolate settings.
column3: The Retired Emperor's tomb (hills north of the capital)

column2:
Genji's great four-quarter mansion (the Rokujō-in) and the single most poetically productive site in the tale — about 148 poems across Ch21–49. Genji builds it in Otome (Ch21) on four adjoining parcels 'in the vicinity of Kyōgoku and Rokujō' on 'the eastern edge of the capital' (Ch21, p.401) — a sixteen-chō block on Sixth Avenue in the southeast of the city that absorbs the former villa of the late Rokujō lady, land the Umetsubo Empress (Akikonomu) had inherited from her mother (Ch21, p.401). It lies near Genji's original Nijō villa and, generations later, serves as the capital anchor of the Uji chapters.

Its defining conceit is that the four quadrants embody the four seasons, each assigned to one of Genji's ladies (Ch21, p.402): Genji and Murasaki hold the southeast (spring); the Umetsubo Empress the southwest (autumn), on 'the former residence of her mother'; Hanachirusato the northeast (summer); and the Akashi lady the northwest (winter). The narrator dwells on each garden — Murasaki's spring quarter 'notable for its tall hills' and planted with red plum, cherry, and wisteria (Ch21, p.402); Akikonomu's autumn quarter of foliage, diverted streams, and a waterfall giving 'the impression of a vista of distant autumn fields' (Ch21, p.402); Hanachirusato's shaded summer quarter, 'creating the effect of being in a mountain retreat' (Ch21, p.403); and the Akashi lady's winter quarter of pines that 'would make a lovely vista when covered with snow' (Ch21, p.403).

As a poetic site, Rokujō is above all the stage of the spring–autumn debate between Murasaki and Akikonomu. In late autumn Akikonomu sends Murasaki flowers and leaves arranged 'in the lid of a box,' borne by a page girl across the garden's arched bridges, with a poem needling that Murasaki's garden merely 'waits for the spring' (Ch21, pp.403–404). Murasaki answers with a miniature landscape — moss, stones, and a small five-needle pine — and a verse on the pine's 'unchanging green' (Ch21, p.404); Genji counsels her to withhold her real riposte until she is 'sheltered by the shade of spring blossoms' (Ch21, p.404). She delivers it in Kochō (Ch24): Genji floats dragon- and heron-prowed boats on the linked southeast–southwest ponds (Ch24, p.439) and sends page girls costumed as birds and butterflies to the autumn quarter with Murasaki's spring reply, whereupon Akikonomu, hearing the dawn music, concedes 'that she had lost the contest between spring and autumn' (Ch24, p.441).

Beyond this signature exchange, the estate generates verse through most of Genji's middle chapters — Tamakazura's suitors and the firefly and picture episodes, the New Year 'first song of spring' (Hatsune, Ch23), and repeated grand musical gatherings across the quadrants — making it, at 148 poems, the densest poetic location in the work. After Genji's death Yūgiri maintains it; it returns in the Uji sequence as the site of Niou's wedding to Roku no Kimi (Yadorigi, Ch49) and of the Akashi Empress's withdrawal (Kagerō, Ch52).

column1: The Rokujō estate (the First Princess's quarters)
column2: The quarters of the First Princess (the Akashi Empress's eldest daughter) and her gentlewomen's salon within the Rokujō estate, prominent only in Kagerō (Ch52, 'The Mayfly'), where the Empress withdraws and holds the Rite of the Eight Lectures 'just when the lotus blossoms were at their peak' (Ch52, p.1105). It is the scene of Kaoru's fateful glimpse of the First Princess: peering through a gap between sliding panels on a sweltering day, he sees her women cracking 'a block of ice' while the Princess, 'in a single white robe of sheer gauze and … holding a piece of ice in her hand,' smiles at their fuss, her hair swept back to reveal features 'beautiful beyond description' (Ch52, p.1106). The salon is also where Kaoru trades the bantering 'maidenflower' verses with the ladies-in-waiting Kosaishō, Chūjō no Omoto, and Ben no Omoto, and where he murmurs the chapter's closing mayfly soliloquy. Part of the larger Rokujō estate (cf. 'The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)').
column3: The Rokujō estate (the First Princess's quarters)

column2: The same Sanjō mansion in its next generation, home to Princess Ōmiya (Aoi's mother, widow of the Minister of the Left) and the setting where her grandchildren Yūgiri and Kumoinokari grow up and fall in love. In Otome (Ch21, 'Maidens of the Dance') the residence hosts an intimate autumn music evening: Tō no Chūjō visits his mother and has Kumoinokari play the koto, and 'as if enticed by the music, leaves from the treetops in the garden fluttered down until none remained' (Ch21, p.382) — the evening on which the childhood romance of Yūgiri and Kumoinokari is exposed and the lovers are separated. The site recurs across the middle chapters (Ch29, 33, 37, 39, 44) as Ōmiya's and later Yūgiri and Kumoinokari's family home. The same Sanjō site as 'The Minister of the Left's residence (Sanjō)' (Ch9) a generation earlier.

The dwelling of a woman Genji had been secretly visiting, near Rokujō and Kyōgoku, in Wakamurasaki (Ch5, 'Little Purple Gromwell'). Setting out on 'a gorgeous moonlit evening' that turns stormy, 'a chill evening rain' falling, he finds her place 'a little too distant,' and on the way passes 'a weather-beaten house standing in the gloomy shade of an ancient grove of trees' (Ch5, p.101). The two poems (05xx) belong to this brief, rain-dampened secret visit. A minor, lightly-sketched episode; the text lingers on the weather and mood rather than the residence.

The Emperor's residential hall (the same site as 'Seiryōden,' Ch1). Its most poetically consequential moment falls in Yadorigi (Ch49, 'Trees Encoiled in Vines of Ivy'), when the reigning Emperor resolves to give Kaoru his Second Princess: 'The chrysanthemums in the garden in front of the Seiryōden were now in full bloom and gorgeously tinted by autumn frosts' (Ch49, p.938) as he plays Go with his daughter and then, over a misty-rain afternoon, challenges Kaoru to a match with the Princess herself as the wager. The chrysanthemum go-wager verses (49YD01/02) are composed here; the hall thus frames the marriage that draws Kaoru back into court life.

The Shishinden (the Hall of State), the palace's principal ceremonial hall and the setting of Hana no en (Ch8, 'A Banquet Celebrating Cherry Blossoms'): 'the Emperor sponsored a banquet to celebrate the viewing of the cherry blossoms in the courtyard on the south side of the Shishinden,' with screens set for 'his newly installed Empress, Fujitsubo, who sat to his right, and for the Crown Prince, who sat on his left' (Ch8, p.153). Genji dances and composes a Chinese poem so admired that the lector can barely read it for the crowd's interruptions and praise (Ch8, p.153). The banquet is the chapter's hinge: leaving it, Genji drifts toward the Kokiden and his fateful first meeting with Oborozukiyo.

column1: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
column2: Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence after his abdication. Its most consequential appearance is in Wakana (Ch34, 'Early Herbs'), where Suzaku — preparing to retreat to his new mountain temple 'in the hills west of the capital' — plans the Third Princess's coming-of-age ceremony, 'when she would don her first train,' and frets over her future: 'After I abandon this worldly life and retreat into the mountains, she'll be left behind' (Ch34, p.566). Here he resolves to entrust the Third Princess to Genji, setting the tale's late tragedy in motion. The text dwells on Suzaku's paternal anxiety rather than on the building. Same site as 'The Retired Emperor's palace (Suzaku's residence)' (Ch17); distinct from Suzaku's mountain temple, his religious retreat.
column3: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)

column1: The Uji villa (the Eighth Prince's residence)
column2: The mountain estate at Uji on the southeast margins of the capital — the descriptive and poetic heart of the final ten chapters (88 poems, Ch45–53), more richly evoked than any other location in the tale. It is the retreat of Hachinomiya, the Eighth Prince (Genji's estranged half-brother), who, after his capital residence burns and his court fortunes collapse, 'moved to an elegant country estate he owned on the outskirts of the capital at a place called Uji' (Hashihime, Ch45, 'The Divine Princess at Uji Bridge,' p.829). Its defining feature is the river: 'His villa at Uji was situated near a weir constructed of wooden posts and netting... This barricade increased the noise of the rapids, making the location less than ideal for a man who wanted to go into quiet retreat' (Ch45, p.829). Yet 'cherry blossoms, the autumn foliage, and the sounds of the river—these were the things that comforted his heart' (Ch45, p.829). The place-name itself resonates: a translator's note ties Uji, via a Kisen poem, to ushi, 'sorrow' — the 'mountain of sorrows,' with 'otherworldly associations.' Beside the grand capital houses the villa is austere, its 'wickerwork screens, which gave off the feel of being in a mountain village,' being 'distinctively simple' (Shiigamoto, Ch46, p.848). It stages the tale's most atmospheric scenes: Kaoru's first moonlit visit, when, peering through a wattle-fence gate, he sees 'the two sisters sitting just inside their raised blinds' across a 'misty garden, enchanting in the pale light of the moon' (Ch45, p.834), and the younger sister toys with a biwa plectrum — 'I may not have a fan, but I can still call forth the moon with this' (Ch45, p.835). The rapids are the villa's emotional keynote, 'drawing forth her tears' so that grief seems to merge with 'the current of the river' (Agemaki, Ch47, p.876); at dawn Niou takes in 'the inhospitable roar of the rapids,' 'the ancient bridge spanning the Uji River,' and 'banks every bit as wild and rough as the sound of the waves' (Ch47, p.897). Across the sequence the villa is where Hachinomiya dies (Ch46), where Ōigimi wastes away and dies (Ch47), where Naka no Kimi is wed and then leaves for the capital (Ch48), where Kaoru installs Ukifune (Ch50), and where, deceived between Kaoru and Niou, Ukifune resolves to drown herself in the river below (Ch51). Kaoru's farewell verse on its cypress pillar closes the villa's story (Tenarai, Ch53).
column3: The Uji villa (the Eighth Prince's residence)

column1: The Umetsubo (Akikonomu's chambers, the plum courtyard)
column2: The plum courtyard, palace residence of Akikonomu when she enters court as consort, from which she takes the name 'Umetsubo': fine ladies-in-waiting 'gathered at the Umetsubo, the chambers of the plum courtyard, so that the atmosphere of her salon was second to none' (E-awase, Ch17, 'A Contest of Illustrations,' p.319). The Umetsubo is the heart of the picture contest: its salon 'focused on collecting examples from tales of old that had the highest reputation' against the Kokiden lady's modern illustrations (Ch17, p.322), and Fujitsubo formally divides the gentlewomen into 'the Left' (Umetsubo) and 'the Right' (Kokiden) 'in the manner of a poetry contest' (Ch17, p.322). The chambers recur in Wakana ge (Ch34) as the now-Empress Akikonomu's base for ceremonial gifts and correspondence.
column3: The Umetsubo (Akikonomu's chambers, the plum courtyard)

column1: The Urin'in Temple (north of the capital)
column2: The Urin'in, a temple north of the capital where Genji goes on retreat in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'): 'he decided to go on an excursion to view autumn fields and to pay a call on the Urin'in Temple north of the capital,' staying 'two or three days at the residence where his maternal uncle was secluded as a master priest,' passing the time 'reading sutras, performing religious devotions, and undergoing many profound and moving experiences' (Ch10, p.206). From here — close to the Kamo Shrine — he sends his covert letter to the Kamo Priestess Asagao. The four poems (10xx) are his autumn-retreat verses. Cf. 'The Kamo Shrine.'
column3: The Urin'in Temple (north of the capital)

column1: The Vice Governor of Hitachi's residence
column2: The wealthy capital residence of the Vice Governor of Hitachi, Ukifune's stepfather, where she is raised as a neglected stepdaughter, in Azumaya (Ch50, 'A Hut in the Eastern Provinces'). Though not 'intrinsically vulgar' — his forebears 'had been high-ranking officials' — the Vice Governor, being 'quite wealthy,' lived 'in a stylish, beautifully appointed residence and pursuing elegant pastimes' (Ch50, p.997), yet retained 'a peculiarly rough quality to his demeanor' and a slight accent from his upbringing in the Eastern Provinces (Ch50, p.997). The residence is the setting of the bush-clover exchange between Chūjō no Kimi and the jilting Lieutenant (Ch50); in Ukifune (Ch51) the mother receives her daughter's farewell poem here while confined by another daughter's childbirth. The house embodies the tension between provincial wealth and courtly refinement that shadows Ukifune's origins.
column3: The Vice Governor of Hitachi's residence

column1: The barrier gate at Ōsaka (Ōsaka no seki)
column2: The Ōsaka barrier gate on the road east, from which the Rokujō lady sends her reply as she journeys to Ise, in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). Answering Genji's farewell poem (delivered as her procession passed his Nijō villa), 'her reply, which she sent from the barrier gate at Ōsaka, did not reach him until the following day' (Ch10, p.197). The single poem (10SK09) is this delayed reply, sent by messenger from the barrier — the last of their exchanges before she leaves the capital. Distinct from the Ōsaka Barrier crossing of Ch16 (Sekiya).
column3: The barrier gate at Ōsaka (Ōsaka no seki)

column2: The Akashi lady's boat as it leaves Akashi for the Ōi villa near the capital, in Matsukaze (Ch18, 'Wind in the Pines'). Persuaded at last to move, the lady travels 'discreetly by boat, and, at around eight in the morning... they set sail,' as 'the achingly beautiful morning mists over the bay... obscured the boat as it rowed away' (Ch18, p.333). Her mother the nun, leaving her husband the Priest behind forever, composes a parting verse likening herself to one 'rowed out to sea upon a fishing boat... a nun now, focused on that distant shore' (Ch18, p.333). The two poems are this sorrowful embarkation, the Priest left gazing after the boat 'as if his very spirit had left his body.'

The capital home of the Middle Captain (Chūjō) — the former son-in-law of the younger nun at Ono — from which he conducts his courtship of Ukifune in Tenarai (Ch53, 'Practicing Calligraphy'). Visiting Ono on his trips to see his priest brother on Mount Hiei (Ch53, p.1133), he glimpses the rescued Ukifune and, taken with her, presses his suit through the nun and by letter from the capital — attentions Ukifune, resolved on renunciation, steadily rebuffs. The four poems (53TN09–10, 18–19) are this one-sided courtship exchange. The text dwells on his visits and letters rather than describing his residence, which serves chiefly as the origin-point of his messages to Ono. (Source-name note: this 'Middle Captain'/Chūjō corresponds to the source label 'Sakon no Shōshō,' a rank discrepancy already flagged for review; he is distinct from the Ch50 jilting Lieutenant who shares that source label.)

column1: The former Hitachi Deputy's residence (in the capital)
column2: The capital residence of Utsusemi (Genji's 'lady of the molted cicada shell') and her much older husband, the former Vice Governor of Iyo and then of Hitachi, in Sekiya (Ch16, 'The Barrier Gate'). After his four-year term in the provinces the couple return to the capital; on his deathbed the old man begs his sons to 'let her do as her heart desires' (Ch16, p.316). But his son the Governor of Kawachi, long attracted to her, presses 'advances that exposed his shockingly base intentions' (Ch16, p.316), and rather than face scandal Utsusemi quietly 'became a nun' (Ch16). (Note: the source label reads 'Hitachi Deputy'; Washburn's text calls the husband the 'Vice Governor of Hitachi.') The residence is undescribed; its significance is as the site of the harassment that drives Utsusemi to take vows.
column3: The former Hitachi Deputy's residence (in the capital)

column1: The house across the Uji River (the Tachibana hideaway)
column2: A rough, half-built house on the far side of the Uji River where Niou hides Ukifune for two snowbound days in Ukifune (Ch51, 'A Boat Cast Adrift'). It stood 'on a manor belonging to Tokikata's uncle, the Governor of Inaba,' and was 'still quite rough and incomplete,' furnished with 'rustic wickerwork screens and the like that did almost nothing to keep the wind out,' while 'patches of snow were melting around the base of the fence' and new snow drifted down (Ch51, p.1059). From it Niou can see, 'through breaks in the mist, the tops of the trees at the Uji villa' across the water. The hideaway's cramped rusticity forces the lovers into unusual intimacy — and its plainness, like Ukifune's own provincial upbringing, quietly measures the gulf between her and the princely Niou. Distinct from Yūgiri's grand across-river estate of Ch46.
column3: The house across the Uji River (the Tachibana hideaway)

column1: The household in Hizen Province (Tamakazura's Kyūshū home)
column2: The household in Hizen Province, Kyūshū, where Tamakazura is raised in obscurity by her nurse's family after her mother Yūgao's death, in Tamakazura (Ch22, 'A Lovely Garland'). 'Now almost twenty and reaching the prime of her beauty, she lived in Hizen Province' (Ch22, p.408), besieged by unwanted suitors. Chief among them is the fearsome local warlord the Audit Officer (Taifu no Gen), whose 'unpleasantly coarse, direct manner was appalling,' his 'accent so thick that his speech was practically incomprehensible' (Ch22, p.408). The two poems (22xx) belong to his crude courtship — the threat that drives the family to flee back to the capital. The household marks the rustic 'hinterlands' upbringing that shadows the aristocratic Tamakazura.
column3: The household in Hizen Province (Tamakazura's Kyūshū home)

column2: The villa of Genji's late father, the Retired Emperor, in the weeks after his death, in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). His widows and concubines 'remained at his villa until the final memorial service on the forty-ninth day... After that they began to drift away one by one, like falling leaves, returning to their family residences' (Ch10, p.198). As Fujitsubo prepares to withdraw to her Sanjō home, 'the interior of the late Emperor's villa had grown hushed and deserted' amid swirling snow (Ch10, p.199); her brother Prince Hyōbu gazes at a snow-bowed five-needle pine and composes a poem (Ch10, p.199). The three poems (10SK11–13) are this melancholy dispersal — a great house emptying as an era ends.

The festival viewing-ground on Ichijō Avenue, site of the famous carriage quarrel in Aoi (Ch9, 'Leaves of Wild Ginger'). Arriving late for the Kamo Festival, the men escorting Genji's pregnant wife shove aside carriages 'that had no guardsmen protecting them' — among them the incognito Rokujō lady's — relegating hers 'to a place behind them, where she could neither see nor be seen' (Ch9, p.163). 'Because the stands for her carriage shafts had been broken in the melee, they had to be propped up on the wheel hubs of some unknown carriages next to hers,' leaving her 'mortified' (Ch9, p.164). When Genji passes in the procession he goes 'coldly... without so much as a glance in her direction' (Ch9, p.165), deepening the humiliation that turns her jealousy murderous. Note: the poem catalogued here (09AO01) is in fact Genji's affectionate hair-trimming verse for Murasaki at Nijō on the festival morning (Ch9, p.166) — the quarrel scene itself carries no poem; the composition site differs from the quarrel and may warrant review.

column1: The mountain temple above Uji (the ascetic's retreat)
column2: The mountain retreat of the saintly ascetic (ajari), a 'master of esoteric Buddhism' who 'lived in the mountains at Uji' and, learning that Hachinomiya was practicing nearby, 'visited regularly' to guide his devotions (Hashihime, Ch45, 'The Divine Princess at Uji Bridge,' p.829). It is where the Eighth Prince goes into final retreat and dies (Ch46), and the ascetic helps arrange the memorial rites (Ch47). Its own poetic moment comes in Sawarabi (Ch48, 'Early Fiddlehead Greens'): the ascetic 'sent a letter to the Uji villa from his mountain retreat,' attached to 'a lovely basket filled with fiddlehead greens and horsetail shoots' — 'These are the first greens of spring, which our acolytes picked for memorial offerings' (Ch48, p.925) — the gift that gives the chapter its title and prompts Naka no Kimi's reply. Distinct from the Eighth Prince's villa below on the riverbank.
column3: The mountain temple above Uji (the ascetic's retreat)

column2: The Ono home of the Bishop of Yokawa's family — his aged mother and his widowed younger sister, both nuns — at the foot of Mount Hiei, and the setting of most of Tenarai (Ch53, 'Practicing Calligraphy'; 28 poems across Ch53–54). Ukifune is not found here but near Uji: returning from a Hatsuse pilgrimage, the nuns shelter at 'an old imperial villa formerly used by the late Emperor Suzaku... just north of the Uji River' (Ch53, p.1121), and there discover her unconscious beneath a great tree behind the ruined hall. Revived, she is brought to the nuns' Ono residence, whose gentleness the narrator sets pointedly against Uji: 'The burbling mountain stream here was far more soothing than the rapids near the old villa at Uji. The Ono residence was elegantly designed, the trees surrounding it were more openly spaced and pleasant, and the flowers in the garden had been meticulously arranged to charmingly stylish effect' (Ch53, p.1131). Here Ukifune passes her days practicing calligraphy (tenarai, the chapter's title), fends off the courtship of the Middle Captain (the younger nun's former son-in-law), and at last takes Buddhist vows. In Yume no ukihashi (Ch54, 'A Floating Bridge in a Dream') Kaoru, having learned she is alive, hears the Bishop describe it as 'a peculiar, rustic abode' where his elderly mother lives as a nun (Ch54, p.1166), and sends his final, unanswered letter-poem to Ukifune here (54YU01). Distinct from the Ch39 Ono villa of the Second Princess.

column1: The nurse's residence (a ruined estate in the capital)
column2: The ruined capital home of the young widow whom Genji recruits as wet-nurse for his infant Akashi daughter, in Miotsukushi (Ch14, 'Channel Markers'). Daughter of a former imperial lady-in-waiting and a Consultant, both now dead, she lives 'in considerably reduced circumstances' (Ch14, p.283): 'her residence, an enormous structure located within a stand of trees that lent the surroundings an indescribably eerie atmosphere, had fallen to ruin' (Ch14, p.284). Genji visits to persuade her, half-teasing, 'I just might change my mind and keep you here for myself.' The two poems (14MT01–02) are this recruitment exchange, in which she gently accuses him of using her only as a pretext 'to follow the one you really long to see' — the Akashi lady. She is then sent off in secret to Akashi.
column3: The nurse's residence (a ruined estate in the capital)

column2: A generic palace setting spanning two widely separated moments (5 poems, Ch8 and Ch43). In Hana no en (Ch8, 'A Banquet Celebrating Cherry Blossoms') it carries a court poem in the banquet's aftermath. In Kōbai (Ch43, 'Red Plum') it hosts the plum-branch courtship by which the Azechi Major Counselor Kōbai woos Niou for his daughter: 'Because Niou is fond of plum blossoms, I sent him a branch from the tree near the veranda on the east side—the one that's blooming so magnificently' (Ch43, p.798), the exchange trading on Niou's celebrated fragrance. The text treats the palace here as a familiar backdrop rather than describing it.
