The palace as the site of Empress Fujitsubo's decision to renounce the world, in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). Finding the court unbearable under the hostile Kokiden Consort after the Retired Emperor's death, and fearing for her young son, she resolves, 'I shall abdicate my title as Empress' (Ch10, p.205); on a last visit her six-year-old is 'warm and familiar around her' as she steels herself to take vows (Ch10, p.205). The passage dwells on her inner turmoil rather than on the palace's appearance.

The palace scene of Genji's reconciliation with Emperor Suzaku after his return from exile, in Akashi (Ch13, 'The Lady at Akashi'). 'A summons from Suzaku arrived, and Genji went to the palace,' where the two 'conversed quietly well into the night' beneath a magnificent full moon 'on this fifteenth day of the month,' until 'Suzaku dissolved in tears' (Ch13, p.278). The reunion verses (13AK26/27) pass here; the emphasis falls on the emotional weight of the meeting rather than on the setting.

Niou's palace quarters, where he is kept under watch after his secret Uji affair becomes known, in Agemaki (Ch47, 'A Bowknot Tied in Maiden's Loops'). As a retainer reports, 'Prince Niou has been confined to the palace and will not be allowed to make any more secret trips,' while His Majesty arranges his marriage to the Minister's daughter (Ch47, p.909). His winter letters to Naka no Kimi at Uji (47AM22/23) issue from this confinement. The place is defined by its function as a gilded restraint, not by description.

Oborozukiyo's palace quarters in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'), after she rises to Principal Handmaid and is 'moved to the brighter, livelier Kokiden' (Ch10, p.200). Though married to Emperor Suzaku, she 'continued her highly secretive correspondence' with Genji (Ch10, p.200), and their affair — conducted here and in the nearby passageway — helps precipitate his exile. Her poem to Genji (10SK24/25) issues from these quarters (Ch10, p.211). Cf. 'The palace (the passageway near the Kokiden).'

Tamakazura's palace quarters during her brief service as Principal Handmaid, in Makibashira (Ch31, 'A Pillar of Cypress'). Emperor Reizei, smitten, seeks her out — 'Emperor Reizei's face in the moonlight was fair beyond description. He looked just like Genji' (Ch31, p.529) — and composes for her in person a verse of frustrated longing (Ch31, p.531), lamenting that her marriage to Higekuro has forestalled his hopes. She soon 'had to give up going to the palace' (Ch31). Washburn's text does not name the Shōkyōden hall as such, and the specific quarters are not described in the translation.

The palace as the site of the Ceremony of Parting before the new High Priestess (Akikonomu) departs for Ise, in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). Her mother, the Rokujō lady, accompanies her; returning to a palace she had 'last come here borne in a palanquin when she was to have become Empress,' she is 'reminded of the mutability of all things in this world' and, 'upon seeing the ninefold palace again,' composes a poem to herself (Ch10, p.196; 10SK07). The scene turns on memory and lost imperial hopes rather than on the ceremony's setting.

The chambers of the First Princess (the Akashi Empress's eldest daughter) at the palace, in Agemaki (Ch47, 'A Bowknot Tied in Maiden's Loops'). On a rainy winter day 'Niou went to the chambers of his sister, the First Princess, just as she and a few of her ladies-in-waiting were quietly looking over some drawings' (Ch47, p.907); catching a glimpse of her profile as her hair 'gently cascaded down,' he wishes 'that he were not as closely related to her.' He collects illustrations from her to send to Naka no Kimi at Uji (47AM21). The passage describes the intimacy of the scene, not the room.

column1: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)
column2: An informal round of the picture contest held before Fujitsubo at the palace, in E-awase (Ch17, 'A Contest of Illustrations'), preliminary to the formal contest before Emperor Reizei. Relaxing her religious austerities to look at the illustrations, Fujitsubo 'divided them into two sides, the Left and the Right, in the manner of a poetry contest' (Ch17, p.322) — the Left drawn from the Umetsubo's gentlewomen, the Right from the Kokiden's — enjoying 'their clever, distinctive styles of argumentation.' The debate verses (17EA05–07) are composed here. Cf. 'The Umetsubo' and 'The Kokiden.'
column3: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)

column1: The palace (the passageway near the Kokiden)
column2: The narrow palace passageway near the Kokiden where Genji and Oborozukiyo first met, and where they resume their affair in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). During Suzaku's ritual abstinence her attendant Chūnagon 'managed to arrange a tryst for them in that narrow passageway where they had first met long ago' (Ch10, p.201), 'nervewracking' because they were 'closer to the veranda than usual' with people about. The verses 10SK14/15 pass here; the place is defined by risk and clandestine memory. Cf. 'The palace (Oborozukiyo's quarters)' and 'The Kokiden' (Ch8).
column3: The palace (the passageway near the Kokiden)

column1: The road at Akashi (en route to the Lady's villa)
column2: The moonlit shore road at Akashi that Genji rides to reach the Akashi lady's hillside villa, in Akashi (Ch13, 'The Lady at Akashi'). Setting out on horseback on the thirteenth of the eighth month under a near-full moon, 'from the road he could see all around the shore, and the moonlight reflecting off the inlets' recalled to him an old verse about scenes to be viewed with 'dear companions' — which at once 'brought his beloved Murasaki to mind,' so that 'he immediately felt the urge to ride on past the villa and continue on to the capital' (Ch13, p.269). The single poem is the verse he murmurs on the road, 'Take flight, my stallion, with autumn moon reflecting.' A transient site defined by moonlight, longing, and divided desire.
column3: The road at Akashi (en route to the Lady's villa)

column1: The road by the Lower Kamo Shrine
column2: The point on Genji's exile route where his party comes within sight of the Lower Kamo Shrine, in Suma (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'). A young Lesser Captain in his escort — who had once served on the day of the Kamo Purification procession and been 'passed over time and again for promotions' because of his loyalty to Genji — 'catching sight of the Lower Kamo Shrine in the distance... dismounted, took the reins of Genji's mount and composed a verse' (Ch12, p.234): recalling 'those leaves of wild ginger that adorned our caps' on that sacred day, he laments 'How cruel the gods of the Kamo Shrine now seem.' The single poem (12SM11) is his bitter farewell to lost favor. Immediately paired with Genji's own prayer at the shrine (see 'The Kamo Shrine (Tadasu Woods),' 12SM12).
column3: The road by the Lower Kamo Shrine

column1: The shore at Suma (the purification site)
column2: The Suma seashore where Genji performs a purification ritual on the Day of the Serpent, early in the third month, in Suma (Ch12, 'The Coast at Suma'). He 'headed down to the sea,' had 'simple soft blinds erected to create a temporary enclosure,' and summoned a yin-yang diviner; 'a large doll to which all defilements and malign spirits had been transferred was placed in a boat and set adrift on the waves,' and 'watching it float away, Genji was reminded of his own fate' (Ch12, p.252). His two poems (12xx) liken him to 'a ritual doll drifting out into an unknown expanse of sea.' No sooner is the rite done than the great storm breaks: 'the waves broke with terrifying power... with each flash of lightning and crash of thunder, the surface of the sea shimmered like a silk quilt' (Ch12, p.253) — the tempest that will drive Genji on to Akashi.
column3: The shore at Suma (the purification site)

column1: The site of Lady Rokujō's healing rites
column2: The Rokujō lady's residence, where she undergoes prayers and rites during the period when her living spirit is unknowingly tormenting Genji's pregnant wife Aoi, in Aoi (Ch9, 'Leaves of Wild Ginger'). Depressed and 'lying prone in her suffering,' she has her attendants 'commission prayers and rites' (Ch9, p.170). Afterward she is horrified to find proof of her own possession clinging to her: 'her robes reeked of the smell of the poppy seeds that exorcists burn to drive out a lingering spirit. Strangely, the smell would not dissipate... no matter how often she tried washing her hair and changing her robes' (Ch9, p.173) — one of the tale's most uncanny images. The two poems (09AO07–08) belong to this anguished, spirit-haunted interval.
column3: The site of Lady Rokujō's healing rites

column1: The small house near Sanjō (Ukifune's hideaway)
column2: A modest, half-finished house that Chūjō no Kimi (Ukifune's mother) keeps for directional retreats, where she hides Ukifune after Niou's intrusion at Nijō in Azumaya (Ch50, 'The Eastern Cottage'). The narrator marks its humbleness: it is 'a stylish house in the vicinity of Sanjō, but because it was still under construction it was not yet completely furnished' (Ch50, p.1025), and a translator's note observes that its cheap plank door (yarido) lends 'a rustic, countrified atmosphere' underscoring the gulf between Kaoru's rank and Ukifune's. It is near — but pointedly distinct from — Kaoru's own grand Sanjō residence. Kaoru finds Ukifune here and carries her off to Uji; the chapter's title poem (azumaya, 'a hut in the Eastern Provinces') is composed at this house (Ch50, p.1032). The text says little else of its appearance — the point being precisely its plainness.
column3: The small house near Sanjō (Ukifune's hideaway)

column1: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
column2: The temporary purification shrine (Nonomiya) on the plains of Sagano, west of the capital, where the Rokujō lady and her daughter the new Ise High Priestess seclude themselves before departing for Ise — the setting of the celebrated autumn farewell in Sakaki (Ch10, 'A Branch of Sacred Evergreen'). Crossing 'the broad plains of Sagano,' Genji finds 'the autumn flowers were withering, and the lonely sound of the wind in the pine trees harmonized with the rasping cries of insects' (Ch10, p.192). The shrine is austere and holy: 'A scattering of temporary dwellings roofed with wood-plank shingles stood inside a low, fragile, rustic-looking enclosure of brushwood. The torii gate was made of logs that had not been stripped of their bark, creating an atmosphere of solemnity' (Ch10, p.193), the sacred fire glowing faintly, few people about. Here Genji, bearing a branch of sakaki (sacred evergreen) that gives the chapter its name, makes his poignant last visit to the Rokujō lady across a screen. The 6 poems are their farewell exchange, charged with regret and finality.
column3: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)

column1: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
column2: The riverside villa on the Ōi River to which the Akashi lady moves from Akashi, in Matsukaze (Ch18, 'Wind in the Pines') and Usugumo (Ch19, 'A Thin Veil of Clouds'). Once the estate of the Akashi nun's grandfather, the late Prince Nakatsukasa, it had 'gradually fallen into a state of disrepair' (Ch18, p.329) before Genji restores it. Because 'it overlooks the river, the place resembles that residence on the shore at Akashi' (Ch18, p.330): 'the main hall... was plain and simple in design and situated within the shade of an indescribably lovely pine grove on the banks of the river,' its 'resemblance to a mountain village' making 'a beautiful impression' (Ch18, p.330); 'the grove of pines that encircled the estate reminded them of their residence in Akashi. It felt as though they had not moved at all' (Ch18, p.333). The wind in these pines gives the chapter its name. With 12 poems, it is the most poetically active of the outlying villas: here Genji visits the Akashi lady and their little daughter, and here (Ch19) the wrenching decision is made to give the child up to Murasaki in the capital. Near Genji's own Katsura villa (see 'Genji's Katsura villa').
column3: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)

column1: The Ōsaka Barrier (the gatehouse at Ōsaka Pass)
column2: The barrier gate at Ōsaka Pass, where Genji and Utsusemi's party cross paths in Sekiya (Ch16, 'The Barrier Gate'). 'On the very day the Vice Governor was scheduled to reach the barrier gate at Ōsaka Pass on his way home' from Hitachi, Genji was setting out for Ishiyama Temple (Ch16, p.314); Utsusemi's party pulls their carriages 'up the slope just below the barrier gate' and waits 'beneath the cedar trees' to let his procession pass (Ch16, p.314). Genji sends a message to Utsusemi via her younger brother — 'Can you ignore the fact that I came to meet you at the barrier gate today?' (Ch16, p.314). The poem (16SY01), which puns on Ōsaka ('hill of meeting'), passes here by messenger. A chance roadside reunion of former lovers.
column3: The Ōsaka Barrier (the gatehouse at Ōsaka Pass)

column1: Tō no Chūjō's billet at the palace
column2: Tō no Chūjō's on-duty lodging at the palace, where 'he had been waiting to go on duty for the Palace Guard' (Momiji no ga, Ch7, 'An Imperial Celebration of Autumn Foliage,' p.151). It figures in the mock-combat aftermath of the Gen no Naishi farce: after the two friends tear each other's robes, Tō no Chūjō 'wrapped up the piece of cloth from Genji's sleeve and returned it' from his billet with a teasing note (Ch7, p.151), and their reconciling verses pass here. The text describes the billet only as a guardsman's waiting-room; its interest is wholly the byplay between the two rivals.
column3: Tō no Chūjō's billet at the palace

column2: Tō no Chūjō's own mansion (on Nijō, named in Ch39), home to his large brood and a recurring stage across ten chapters (33 poems). Its signal poetic scene is the wisteria banquet of Fuji no uraba (Ch33, 'Shoots of Wisteria Leaves'), by which Tō no Chūjō at last relents and welcomes Yūgiri as Kumoinokari's husband, ending their long-thwarted romance. His garden wisteria 'bloomed in wild profusion at the beginning of the fourth month,' its color 'unusually brilliant this year' (Ch33, p.551), and he stages a music party to show it off, sending Kashiwagi with the invitation to Yūgiri; the men 'moved out to the veranda to admire the wisteria' (Ch33, p.553), where the host murmurs the title-line, 'Shoots of wisteria leaves . . .' (Ch33, p.553), cueing Kashiwagi to break off a long cluster of blossoms and present it. The residence is also where Kashiwagi sickens and dies (Ch34–36). Distinct from his son Kōbai's villa (Ch43).

The Uji River and the villa's environs during Niou's autumn maple-viewing excursion in Agemaki (Ch47, 'A Bowknot Tied in Maiden's Loops'). Around the first of the tenth month Kaoru tempts Niou to Uji 'by remarking how delightful the fishing weir was at this time of year and suggesting that the Prince should go on an excursion to view the autumn foliage' (Ch47, p.902). Word leaks and the outing swells into a grand party: 'the delightful sounds of music could be heard from the boats being punted up and down the river,' and the sisters glimpse through the mist 'the brocadelike thatch of autumn leaves that decorated the roof of the boat' (Ch47, p.902). But a second large party arrives, and the thwarted Niou can only send a letter across to the villa — to which the anxious younger Princess dares not reply (Ch47, p.903), leaving Ōigimi 'even more devastated.' The excursion's poems turn on longing across an uncrossable stretch of water. Distinct from the Ch51 boat crossing and hideaway.

The humble house on Gojō Avenue where Genji first glimpses Yūgao, in Yūgao (Ch4, 'The Lady of the Evening Faces'). Waiting outside his ailing nurse's house next door, he notices 'white flowers... blooming' on a vine over a board fence, and a guard names them: 'Those white flowers, my lord? They're called evening faces. Their name would suggest a person of some consequence, and yet here they are blooming in front of this run-down, rustic dwelling!' (Ch4, p.56). A fan bearing a poem is sent out to him from within (Ch4, p.57), beginning the affair. The four poems (04xx) launch from this fateful, flower-framed encounter with the mysterious lady of Gojō.

The dwelling of Tō no Chūjō's 'wild pink' — the lover he describes in the rainy-night discussion of women in Hahakigi (Ch2, 'Broom Cypress'), later revealed to be Yūgao. He recalls a tender exchange over their little daughter, likening the child to a nadeshiko (wild pink) and the mother to tokonatsu ('never-ending summer'): 'there is none dearer than my little wild pink' (Ch2, p.33). The three poems (02HH05–07) are this tokonatsu/nadeshiko exchange. This is where the lady lived before she was frightened into hiding by Tō no Chūjō's jealous principal wife and vanished — the disappearance that sets up Genji's discovery of her in Ch4. Not physically described; it is the emotional origin-point of the Yūgao story.

The shrine at Ōharano, in the western suburbs, the destination of Emperor Reizei's grand winter excursion in Miyuki (Ch29, 'An Imperial Excursion'): 'In the twelfth month of that year, Emperor Reizei decided to go on an excursion to the shrine at Ōharano in the suburbs west of the capital' (Ch29, p.489). The procession is the most magnificent ever seen, with falconry and hunting parties, as 'a few flakes of snow drifted down, and the cloudy sky took on an elegant glow' (Ch29, p.489). Tamakazura comes to view it, hoping to glimpse her still-unacknowledged father Tō no Chūjō, and is struck that Emperor Reizei — whose face is 'a mirror image of Genji's' — outshines every man present (Ch29, p.491). The two poems (29xx) attach to the excursion, the event that decides Genji to bring Tamakazura to court. (A translator's note ties the scene to the historical Ōharano excursion of Emperor Daigo in 928.)
